style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline;color:#5f6368;display:block;font-size:16px;font-style:italic;margin-top:8px;width:100%\"\u003eTable 6: Detection opportunities for suspicious egress traffic flows\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eVirtualization Infrastructure Protections\u003c/span\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e \u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThreat actors often target virtualization infrastructure (e.g., VMware vSphere, Microsoft Hyper-V) as part of their reconnaissance, lateral movement, data theft, and potential ransomware deployment objectives. Securing virtualization infrastructure requires a Zero Trust network posture as a primary defense. Because management appliances often lack native MFA for local privileged accounts, identity-based security alone can be a high-risk single point of failure. If credentials are compromised, the logical network architecture becomes the final line of defense protecting the virtualization management plane.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo reduce the attack surface of virtualized infrastructure, a best practice for VMware vSphere vCenter ESXi and Hyper-V appliances and servers is to isolate and restrict access to the management interfaces, essentially enclaving these interfaces within isolated virtual local area networks (VLANs) (network segments) where connectivity is only permissible from dedicated subnets where administrative actions can be initiated.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo protect the virtualization control plane, organizations must consider a \"defense-in-depth\" network model. This architecture integrates physical isolation and east-west micro-segmentation to remove all access paths from untrusted networks. The result is a management zone that remains isolated and resilient, even during an active intrusion.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eVMware vSphere Zero-Trust Network Architecture\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe primary goal is to ensure that even if privileged credentials are compromised, the logical network remains the definitive defensive layer preventing access to virtualization management interfaces.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eImmutable VLAN Segmentation\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Enforce strict isolation using distinct 802.1Q VLAN IDs for host management, Infrastructure/VCSA, vMotion (non-routable), Storage (non-routable), and production Guest VMs.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eVirtual Routing and Forwarding (VRF)\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Transition all infrastructure VLANs into a dedicated VRF instance. This ensures that even a total compromise of the \"User\" or \"Guest\" zones results in no available route to the management zone(s).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch6\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eLayer 3 and 4 Access Policies\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h6\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe management network must be accessible only from trusted, hardened sources.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePAW-Exclusive Access:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Deconstruct all direct routes from the general corporate LAN to management subnets. Access must originate strictly from a designated Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) subnet.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIngress Filtering (Management Zone)\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eALLOW:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e TCP/443 (UI/API) and TCP/902 (MKS) from the PAW subnet only.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDENY\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Explicitly block SSH (TCP/22) and VAMI (TCP/5480) from all sources \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eexcept\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e the PAW subnet.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRestrictive Egress Policy:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Enforce outbound filtering at the hardware gateway (as the VCSA GUI cannot manage egress). To prevent persistence using C2 traffic and data exfiltration, block all internet access except to specific, verified update servers (e.g., VMware Update Manager) and authorized identity providers.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch6\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHost-Based Firewall Enforcement\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h6\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eComplement network firewalls with host-level filtering to eliminate visibility gaps within the same VLAN.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eVCSA (Photon OS)\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Transition the default policy to \"Default Deny\" via the VAMI or, preferably, at the OS level using iptables/nftables for granular source/destination mapping. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eESXi Hypervisors: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRestrict all services (SSH, Web Access, NFC/Storage) to specific management IPs by deselecting \"Allow connections from any IP address.\"\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional information related to \u003ca href\u003d\"https://knowledge.broadcom.com/external/article/377036/how-to-block-all-traffic-on-vcenter-exce.htm\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003eVMware vSphere VCSA host based firewalls\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eA \u003ca href\u003d\"https://kb.vmware.com/s/article/1012382\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003elisting of administrative ports\u003c/a\u003e associated with VMWare vCenter (that should be targeted for isolation).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHyper-V Zero-Trust Network Architecture \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSimilar to vSphere, Hyper-V requires strict isolation of its various traffic types to prevent lateral movement from guest workloads to the management plane.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eVLAN Segmentation:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Organizations must enforce isolation using distinct VLANs for Host Management, Live Migration, Cluster Heartbeat (CSV), and Production Guest VMs.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNon-Routable Networks:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Traffic for Live Migration and Cluster Shared Volumes (CSV) should be placed on non-routable VLANs to ensure these high-bandwidth, sensitive streams cannot be intercepted from other segments.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch6\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eLayer 3 and 4 Access Policies\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h6\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe management network must be accessible only from trusted, hardened sources.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePAW-Exclusive Access:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Deconstruct all direct routes from the general corporate LAN to management subnets. Access must originate strictly from a designated Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) subnet.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIngress Filtering (Management Zone)\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eALLOW\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: WinRM / PowerShell Remoting (TCP/5985 and TCP/5986), RDP (TCP/3389), and WMI/RPC (TCP/135 and dynamic RPC ports)strictly from the PAW subnet. If using Windows Admin Center, allow HTTPS (TCP/443) to the gateway.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDENY\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Explicitly block SMB (TCP/445), RPC/WMI (TCP/135), and all other management traffic from untrusted sources to prevent credential theft and lateral movement.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRestrictive Egress Policy: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnforce outbound filtering at the network gateway. To prevent persistence using C2 traffic and data exfiltration, block all internet access from Hyper-V hosts except to specific, verified update servers (e.g., internal WSUS), authorized Active Directory Domain Controllers, and Key Management Servers (KMS).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch6\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHost-Based Firewall Enforcement\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h6\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eUse the Windows Firewall with Advanced Security (WFAS) to achieve a defense-in-depth posture at the host level.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eScope Restriction: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor all enabled management rules (e.g., File and Printer Sharing, WMI, PowerShell Remoting), modify the Remote IP Address scope to \"These IP addresses\" and enter only the PAW and management server subnets.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eManagement Logging: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnable logging for Dropped Packets in the Windows Firewall profile. This allows the SIEM to ingest \"denied\" connection attempts, which serve as high-fidelity indicators of internal reconnaissance or unauthorized access attempts.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional information related to \u003ca href\u003d\"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/it-pro/windows-server-2012-r2-and-2012/jj721516(v\u003dws.11)\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003eHyper-V host based firewalls\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional information related to \u003ca href\u003d\"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/virtualization/hyper-v/plan/plan-hyper-v-security-in-windows-server\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003esecuring Hyper-V\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eGeneral Virtualization Hardening \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo protect management interfaces for VMware vSphere the VMKernel network interface card (NIC) should \u003c/span\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003enot\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e be bound to the same virtual network assigned to virtual machines running on the host. Additionally, ESXi servers can be configured in lockdown mode, which will only allow console access from the vCenter server(s). Additional information related to \u003ca href\u003d\"https://kb.vmware.com/s/article/1008077\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003elockdown mode\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe SSH protocol (TCP/22) provides a common channel for accessing a physical virtualization server or appliance (vCenter) for administration and troubleshooting. Threat actors commonly leverage SSH for direct access to virtualization infrastructure to conduct destructive attacks. In addition to enclaving access to administrative interfaces, SSH access to virtualization infrastructure should be disabled and only enabled for specific use-cases. If SSH is required, network ACLs should be used to limit where connections can originate.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIdentity segmentation should also be configured when accessing administrative interfaces associated with virtualization infrastructure. If Active Directory authentication provides direct integrated access to the physical virtualization stack, a threat actor that has compromised a valid Active Directory account (with permissions to manage the virtualization infrastructure) could potentially use the account to directly access virtualized systems to steal data or perform destructive actions.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAuthentication to virtualized infrastructure should rely upon dedicated and unique accounts that are configured with strong passwords and that are \u003c/span\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003enot\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e co-used for additional access within an environment. Additionally, accessing management interfaces associated with virtualization infrastructure should only be initiated from isolated privileged access workstations, which prevent the storing and caching of passwords used for accessing critical infrastructure components.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eProtecting Hypervisors Against Offline Credential Theft and Exfiltration\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOrganizations should implement a proactive, defense-in-depth technical hardening strategy to systematically address security gaps and mitigate the risk of offline credential theft from the hypervisor layer. The core of this attack is an offline credential theft technique known as a \"Disk Swap.\" Once an adversary has administrative control over the hypervisor (vSphere or Hyper-V), they perform the following steps:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTarget Identification:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e The actor identifies a critical virtualized asset, such as a Domain Controller (DC) \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOffline Manipulation:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e The target VM is powered off, and its virtual disk file (e.g., .vmdk for VMware or .vhd/.vhdx for Hyper-V) is detached.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNTDS.dit Extraction\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: The disk is attached to a staging or \"orphaned\" VM under the attacker's control. From this unmonitored machine, they copy the NTDS.dit Active Directory database.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStealthy Recovery\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: The disk is re-attached to the original DC, and the VM is powered back on, leaving minimal forensic evidence within the guest operating system.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch6\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHardening and Mitigation Guidance\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h6\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo defend against this logic, organizations must implement a defense-in-depth strategy that focuses on cryptographic isolation and strict lifecycle management.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eVirtual Machine Encryption\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Organizations must encrypt all Tier 0 virtualized assets (e.g., Domain Controllers, PKI, and Backup Servers). Encryption ensures that even if a virtual disk file is stolen or detached, it remains unreadable without access to the specific keys. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStrict Decommissioning Processes\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Do not leave powered-off or \"orphaned\" virtual machines on datastores. These \"ghost\" VMs are ideal staging environments for attackers. Formally decommission assets by deleting their virtual disks rather than just removing them from the inventory.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHarden Hypervisor Accounts\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Disable or restrict default administrative accounts (such as root on ESXi or the local Administrator on Hyper-V hosts). Enforce \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://knowledge.broadcom.com/external/article/336894/enabling-or-disabling-lockdown-mode-on-a.html\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eLockdown Mode\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e (VMware ESXi feature) where possible to prevent direct host-level changes outside of the central management plane.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRemote Audit Logging\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Enable and forward all hypervisor-level audit logs (e.g., hostd.log, vpxa.log, or Windows Event Logs for Hyper-V) to a centralized SIEM. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eProtecting Backups\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSecurity measures must encompass both production and backup environments. An attack on the production plane is often coupled with a simultaneous focus on backup integrity, creating a total loss of operational continuity. Virtual disk files (VMDK for VMware and VHD/VHDX for Hyper-V) represent a high-value target for offline data theft and direct manipulation.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch6\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHardening and Mitigation Guidance\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h6\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo mitigate the risk of offline theft and backup manipulation, organizations must implement a \"Default Encrypted\" policy across the entire lifecycle of the virtual disk .\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAt-Rest Encryption for all Tier-0 Assets:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Implement vSphere VM Encryption or Hyper-V Shielded VMs for all critical infrastructure (e.g., Domain Controllers, Certificate Authorities). This ensures that the raw VMDK or VHDX files are cryptographically protected, rendering them unreadable if detached or mounted by an unauthorized party.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEncrypted Backup Repositories\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Ensure that the backup application is configured to encrypt backup data at rest using a unique key stored in a separate, hardened Key Management System (KMS). This prevents \"direct manipulation\" of the backup files even if the backup storage itself is compromised. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNetwork Isolation of Storage \u0026amp; Backups: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIsolate the storage management network and the backup infrastructure into dedicated, non-routable VLANs. Access to the backup console and repositories must require phishing-resistant MFA and originate from a designated Privileged Access Workstation (PAW).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eImmutability and Air-Gapping\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e: Use Immutable Backup Repositories to ensure that once a backup is written, it cannot be modified or deleted by any user including a compromised administrator for a set period. This provides a definitive recovery point in the event of a ransomware attack or intentional data sabotage.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDetection Opportunities for Monitoring Virtualization Infrastructure\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003cdiv System Shutdown/Reboot\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDetect sequences where multiple VMs are powered off within a short time window (e.g., \u0026gt;5 VMs in 10 minutes) via vCenter events. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCorrelate with vpxd.log \"ReceivedPowerOffVM\" events.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eVMDK File Access from Non-Standard Processes\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1486/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1486 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor ESXi shell.log for execution of \"esxcli system settings encryption set\" with \"--require-exec-installed-only\u003dF\" or \"--require-secure-boot\u003dF\". rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCloudflare DDoS style\u003d\"text-align:center\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline;color:#5f6368;display:block;font-size:16px;font-style:italic;margin-top:8px;width:100%\"\u003eTable 8: Common cloud capabilities to mitigate DDoS attacks\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHardening the Cloud Perimeter \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWith the hybrid operating model of modern day infrastructure, cloud consoles and SaaS platforms are high-value targets for credential harvesting and data exfiltration. Minimizing these risks requires a dual-defense strategy: robust identity controls to prevent unauthorized access, and platform-specific guardrails to protect access to resources, data, and to minimize the attack surface. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStrong Authentication Enforcement\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStrong authentication is the foundational requirement for cloud resilience and securing cloud infrastructure. Similar to on-premises environments, a compromise of a privileged credential, token, or session could lead to unintended consequences that result in a high-impact event for an organization. To mitigate these pervasive risks, organizations must unconditionally enforce strong authentication for all external-facing cloud services, administrative portals, and SaaS platforms. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOrganizations should enforce the usage of phishing-resistant authenticators such as FIDO2 (WebAuthn) hardware tokens or passkeys, or certificate based authentication for accounts assigned privileged roles and functions. For non-privileged users, authenticator software (Microsoft Authenticator or Okta Verify) should be configured to utilize device-bound factors such as Windows Hello for Business or TouchID.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditionally, organizations should leverage the concept of authenticators (identity + device attestation) as part of the authentication transaction. This includes enforcing a validated-device access policy that restricts privileged access to only originate from managed, compliant, and healthy devices. Trusted network zones should be defined in order to restrict access to cloud resources from the open internet. Untrusted network zones should be defined to restrict authentication from anonymizing services such as VPNs or TOR. Using device-bound session credentials where possible mitigates the risk of session token theft.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIdentity and Device Segmentation for Privileged Actions\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe implementation of privileged access workstations (PAWs) is a critical defense against threat actors attempting to compromise administrative sessions. A PAW is a highly hardened, dedicated hardware endpoint used exclusively for sensitive administrative tasks.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdministrators should leverage a non-privileged account for daily tasks, while privileged actions are restricted to only being permissible from the hardened PAW, or from explicitly defined IP ranges. This \"air-gap\" between communication and administration prevents an adversary from moving laterally from a compromised non-privileged identity to a privileged context within hybrid environments. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eJust-in-Time Access and the Principle of Least Privilege\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStatic, standing privileges present a security risk in hybrid environments. Following a zero-trust cloud architecture, administrative privileges should be entirely ephemeral. Implementing Just-In-Time (JIT) and Just-Enough-Access (JEA) mechanisms ensures that administrators are granted only the specific, granular permissions necessary to perform a discrete task, and only for a highly limited duration, after which the permissions are automatically revoked. This architectural model provides organizations with the ability to enforce approvals for privileged actions, enhanced monitoring, and detailed visibility regarding any privileged actions taken within a specific session.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSecuring Non-Human Identities\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOrganizations should implement identity governance practices that include processes to rotate API keys, certificates, service account secrets, tokens, and sessions on a predefined basis. AI agents or identities correlating to autonomous outcomes should be configured with strictly scoped permissions and associated monitoring. Non-privileged users should be restricted from authorizing third-party application integrations or creating API keys without organizational approval.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eContinuous scanning should be performed to identify and remediate hard-coded secrets and sensitive credentials across all cloud and SaaS environments.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStorage Infrastructure Security and Immutable Backups\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe strategic objective of a destructive cyberattack—whether for extortion or sabotage—is to prolong recovery and reconstitution efforts by ensuring data is irrecoverable. Modern adversaries systematically target the backup plane as part of a destructive event. If backups remain mutable or share an identity plane with the primary environment, attackers can delete or encrypt them, transforming an incident into a prolonged and chaotic recovery exercise.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWhile modern-day redundancy for backups should include multiple data copies across diverse media, geographic separation can be a subverted defensive strategy if logical access is unified. To ensure resilience against destructive attacks, the secondary recovery environment should reside within a sovereign cloud tenant or isolated subscription. This environment should be governed by an independent Identity and Access Management (IAM) plane, using distinct credentials and administrative personas that share no commonality with the production environment.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eBackups within an isolated environment must be anchored by immutable storage architectures. By leveraging hardware-verified Write-Once, Read-Many (WORM) technology, the recovery plane ensures that data integrity is mathematically guaranteed. Once committed, data cannot be modified, encrypted, or deleted—even by accounts with root or global administrative privileges, until the retention period expires. This creates a definitive \"fail-safe\" that ensures a known-good recovery point remains accessible regardless of potential security risks in the primary environment.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional defense-in-depth security architecture controls relevant to common cloud-based infrastructures are included in Table 9.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003cdiv align\u003d\"left\"\u003e\n\u003cdiv Protections\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEndpoint Hardening\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWindows Firewall Configurations\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOnce initial access to on-premises infrastructure is established, threat actors will conduct lateral movement to attempt to further expand the scope of access and persistence. To protect Windows endpoints from being accessed using common lateral movement techniques, a Windows Firewall policy can be configured to restrict the scope of communications permitted between endpoints within an environment. A Windows Firewall policy can be enforced locally or centrally as part of a Group Policy Object (GPO) configuration. At a minimum, the common ports and protocols leveraged for lateral movement that should be blocked between workstation-to-workstation and workstations to non-domain controllers and non-file servers include:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSMB (TCP/445, TCP/135, TCP/139)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRemote Desktop Protocol (TCP/3389)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWindows Remote Management (WinRM)/Remote PowerShell (TCP/80, TCP/5985, TCP/5986)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWindows Management Instrumentation (WMI) (dynamic port range assigned through Distributed Component Object Model (DCOM))\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eUsing a GPO (Figure 5), the settings listed in Table 11 can be configured for the Windows Firewall to control \u003c/span\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003einbound\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e communications to endpoints in a managed environment. The referenced settings will effectively block all inbound connections for the \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePrivate\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e and \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePublic\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e profiles, and for the \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNTLM Authentication Configurations\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThreat actors often attempt to harvest credentials (including Windows NTLMv1 hashes) based upon outbound SMB or WebDAV communications. Organizations should review NTLM settings for Windows-based endpoints, and work to harden, disable, or restrict NTLMv1 authentication requests. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo fully restrict NTLM authentication to remote servers, the following GPO settings can be leveraged:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eComputer Configuration \u0026gt; Windows Settings \u0026gt; Security Settings \u0026gt; Local Policies \u0026gt; Security Options \u0026gt; Network Security: Restrict NTLM: Outgoing NTLM traffic to remote servers \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAllow all\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAudit all\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDeny all\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNote:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIf \"\u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDeny all\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\" is selected, the client computer cannot authenticate (send credentials) to a remote server using NTLM authentication. Before setting to \"\u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDeny all,\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\" organizations should configure the GPO setting with the \"\u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAudit all\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\" enforcement. With this configuration, audit and block events will be recorded within the Operational event log on endpoints (\u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eApplications and Services Log\\Microsoft\\Windows\\NTLM\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIf any recorded NTLM authentication events are required, organizations can configure the \"\u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNetwork security: Restrict NTLM: Add remote server exceptions for NTLM authentication\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\" setting to define a listing of remote servers, which are required to use NTLM authentication.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan named pipe access, and PrinterBug via SpoolService RPC calls.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003c/tbody\u003e\n\u003c/table\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003cdiv communications\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRemote Desktop Protocol Hardening\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRemote Desktop Protocol (RDP) is a common method used by threat actors to remotely connect to systems, laterally move from the perimeter onto a larger scope of internal systems, and perform malicious activities (such as data theft or ransomware deployment). External-facing systems with RDP open to the internet present an elevated risk. Threat actors may exploit this vector to gain initial access to an organization and then perform lateral movement into the organization to complete their mission objectives.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eProactively, organizations should scan their public IP address ranges to identify systems with RDP (TCP/3389) and other protocols (SMB – TCP/445) open to the internet. At a minimum, RDP and SMB should not be directly exposed for ingress and egress access to/from the internet. If required for operational purposes, explicit controls should be implemented to restrict the source IP addresses, which can interface with systems using these protocols. The following hardening recommendations should also be implemented.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnforce Multi-Factor Authentication\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIf external-facing RDP must be used for operational purposes, MFA should be enforced when connecting using this method. This can be accomplished either via the integration of a third-party MFA technology or by leveraging a Remote Desktop Gateway and Azure Multifactor Authentication Server using Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service (\u003ca href\u003d\"https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/authentication/howto-mfaserver-nps-rdg\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003eRADIUS\u003c/a\u003e)\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eLeverage Network-Level Authentication\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor external-facing RDP servers, Network-Level Authentication (NLA) provides an extra layer of preauthentication before a connection is established. NLA can also be useful for protecting against brute-force attacks, which often target open internet-facing RDP servers.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNLA can be configured either via the user interface (UI) (Figure 10) or via Group Policy (Figure 11).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e"]]],[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,null,[[[null,"\u003cp\u003eFigure 10: Enabling NLA via the UI\u003c/p\u003e"],["https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig10.max-1100x1100.png","https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig10.max-1100x1100.png 1060w, https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig10.max-1100x1100.png 1074w"," 1060px,  1074px","https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig10.max-1100x1100.png"],null,2]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eUsing a GPO, the setting for NLA can be configured via:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eComputer Configuration \u0026gt; Policies \u0026gt; Administrative Templates \u0026gt; Windows Components \u0026gt; Remote Desktop Services \u0026gt; Remote Desktop Session Host \u0026gt; Security \u0026gt; Require user authentication for remote connections by using Network Level Authentication\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnabled\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e"]]],[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,null,[[[null,"\u003cp\u003eFigure 11: Enabling NLA via Group Policy\u003c/p\u003e"],["https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig11.max-600x600.png","https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig11.max-600x600.png 599w, https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig11.max-600x600.png 599w"," 599px,  599px","https://storage.googleapis.com/gweb-cloudblog-publish/images/destructive-attacks-guidance-fig11.max-600x600.png"],null,3]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSome caveats about leveraging NLA for RDP:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe Remote Desktop client v7.0 (or greater) must be leveraged.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNLA uses CredSSP to pass authentication requests on the initiating system. CredSSP stores credentials in Local Security Authority (LSA) memory on the initiating system, and these credentials may remain in memory even after a user logs off the system. This provides a potential exposure risk for credentials in memory on the source system.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOn the RDP server, users permitted for remote access using RDP must be assigned the \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAccess this computer from the network\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e privilege when NLA is enforced. \u003c/span\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThis privilege is often explicitly denied for user accounts to protect against lateral movement techniques.\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRestrict Administrative Accounts from Leveraging RDP on Internet-Facing Systems\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor external-facing RDP servers, highly privileged domain and local administrative accounts should not be permitted access to authenticate with the external-facing systems using RDP (Figure 12). \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThis can be enforced using Group Policy, configurable via the following path: \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eComputer Configuration \u0026gt; Policies \u0026gt; Windows Settings \u0026gt; Security Settings \u0026gt; Local Policies \u0026gt; User Rights Assignment \u0026gt; Deny log on through Terminal Services\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e"]]],[null,null,null,null,null,null,null,null,[[[null,"\u003cp\u003eFigure 12: Group Policy configuration for restricting endpoints, additional security hardening technologies and controls should be considered. Examples of additional security controls for consideration for Windows-based endpoints are provided as follows.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWindows Defender Application Control\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWindows Defender Application Control is a set of inherent configuration settings within Active Directory that provide lockdown and control mechanisms for controlling which applications and files users can run on endpoints. A Windows E5 license provides advanced management capabilities for ASR.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional information related to \u003ca href\u003d\"https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/security/defender-endpoint/attack-surface-reduction\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003eMicrosoft Defender Attack Surface Reduction functionality\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eControlled Folder Access\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eControlled folder access can help protect data from being encrypted by ransomware. Beginning with Windows 10 version 1709+ and Windows Server 2019+, controlled folder access was introduced within Windows Defender Antivirus (as part of Windows Defender Exploit Guard). \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOnce controlled folder access is enabled, applications and executable files are assessed by Windows Defender Antivirus, which then determines if an application is malicious or safe. If an application is determined to be malicious or suspicious, it will be blocked from making changes to any files in a protected folder.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOnce enabled, controlled folder access will apply to a number of system folders and default locations, Tamper protection either in Windows (via Microsoft Defender for Endpoint) or integrated within third-party AV/EDR platforms can help protect security tools from being modified or stopped by a threat actor. Organizations should review the configuration of security technologies that are deployed to endpoints and verify if tamper protection is (or can be) enabled to protect against unauthorized modification. protection.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional information related to \u003ca DCSync.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003c/tbody\u003e\n\u003c/table\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003cdiv style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline;color:#5f6368;display:block;font-size:16px;font-style:italic;margin-top:8px;width:100%\"\u003eTable 20: Detection opportunities for privileged accounts, groups, and GPO modifications\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePrivileged and Service Account Protections\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003ch5\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIdentify and Review Noncomputer Accounts Configured with an SPN\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h5\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAccounts with service principal names (SPNs) are commonly targeted by threat actors for privilege escalation. Using Kerberos, any domain user can request a Kerberos service ticket (TGS) from a domain controller for any account configured with an SPN. Noncomputer accounts likely are configured with guessable (nonrandom) passwords. Regardless of the domain function level or the host's Windows version, SPNs that are registered under a noncomputer account will use the legacy RC4-HMAC encryption suite rather than Advanced Encryption Standard (AES). The key used for encryption and decryption of the RC4-HMAC encryption type represents an unsalted NTLM hash version of the account's password, which could be derived via cracking the ticket.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOrganizations should review Active Directory to identify noncomputer accounts configured with an SPN. Noncomputer accounts correlated to registered SPNs are likely service accounts and provide a method for a threat actor (without administrative privileges) to potentially derive (crack) the plain-text password for the account (Kerberoasting). To identify noncomputer accounts configured with an SPN, the PowerShell cmdlet referenced in Figure 31 can be run from a domain controller.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003cpre\u003e\u003ccode\u003eGet-ADUser -Filter {(ServicePrincipalName -like \"*\")} | Select-Object name,samaccountname,sid,enabled,DistinguishedName\u003c/code\u003e\u003c/pre\u003e\n\u003cp style\u003d\"text-align:center\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline;color:#5f6368;display:block;font-size:16px;font-style:italic;margin-top:8px;width:100%\"\u003eFigure 31: PowerShell cmdlet to identify noncomputer accounts configured with an SPN\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWhere possible, organizations should deregister noncomputer accounts with SPNs configured. Where SPNs are needed, organizations should mitigate the risk associated with Kerberoasting attacks. Accounts with SPNs should be configured with strong, unique passwords (e.g., minimum 25+ characters) with the passwords rotated on a periodic basis for the accounts. Furthermore, privileges should be reviewed and reduced for these accounts to ensure that each account has the minimum required privileges needed for the intended function.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAccounts with SPNs should be considered in-scope for the proactive hardening measures detailed throughout this blog post.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNote:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSPNs should never be associated with regular interactive user accounts.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDetection Opportunities for Noncomputer Accounts Configured with an Restrictions\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePrivileged and service account credentials are commonly used for lateral movement and establishing persistence.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor any accounts that have privileged access throughout an environment, the accounts should not be used on standard workstations and laptops, but rather from designated systems (e.g., privileged access workstations [PAWs]) that reside in restricted and protected VLANs and tiers. Dedicated privileged accounts should be defined for each tier, with controls that enforce that the accounts can only be used within the designated tier. Guardrail enforcement for privileged accounts can be defined within GPOs or by using authentication policy silos (Windows Server 2012 R2 domain-functional level or above).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe recommendations for restricting the scope of access for privileged accounts are based upon Microsoft's guidance for securing privileged access. virtualized desktop infrastructure).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe settings referenced as follows are configurable using user Accounts\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOrganizations with static service accounts should review the feasibility of migrating the service accounts to be managed service accounts (MSAs) or group managed service accounts (gMSAs).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMSAs were first introduced with the Windows Server 2008 R2 Active Directory schema (domain-functional level) and provide automatic password management (30-day rotation) for dedicated service accounts that are associated with running services on specific endpoints.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStandard MSA: The account is associated with a single endpoint, and the complex password for the account is automatically managed and changed on a predefined frequency (30 days by default). While an MSA can only be associated with a single computer account, multiple services on the same endpoint can leverage the MSA.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eGroup managed service account (gMSA): First introduced with Windows Server 2012 and are very similar to MSAs, but allow for a single gMSA to be leveraged across \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003emultiple\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e endpoints.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCommon uses for MSAs and gMSAs:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan MSAs\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNote:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThreat actors can potentially discover accounts and groups that have permissions to read/leverage the password for a gMSA for privilege escalation and lateral movement. This can be accomplished by leveraging the \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eget-adserviceaccount\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e PowerShell cmdlet and enumerating the \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003emsDS-GroupMSAMembership\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e (\u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePrincipalsAllowedToRetrieveManagedPassword\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e) configuration for a gMSA, which stores the security principals that can access the gMSA password. It is important that when configuring managed service accounts, organizations focus on restricting the scope of accounts and groups that have the ability to obtain and leverage the password for the managed service accounts and enforce structured monitoring of these accounts and groups.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor Group\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eBy leveraging the Protected Users security group for privileged accounts, an organization can minimize various exposure factors and common exploitation methods by a threat actor or malware variant obtaining credentials for privileged accounts on disk or in memory from endpoints.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eBeginning with Microsoft Windows 8.1 and Microsoft Windows Server 2012 R2 (and above), the Protected Users security group was introduced to manage credential exposure within an environment. Members of this group automatically have specific protections applied to accounts, including:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe Kerberos ticket granting ticket (TGT) expires after four hours, rather than the normal 10-hour default setting.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNo NTLM hash for an account is stored in LSASS, since only Kerberos authentication is used (NTLM authentication is disabled for an account).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCached credentials are blocked. A domain controller must be available to authenticate the account.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWDigest authentication is disabled for an account, regardless of an endpoint's applied policy settings.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDES and RC4 cannot be used for Kerberos preauthentication (Server 2012 R2 or higher); rather, Kerberos with AES encryption will be enforced.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAccounts cannot be used for either constrained or unconstrained delegation (equivalent to enforcing the \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAccount is sensitive and cannot be delegated\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e setting in Active Directory Users and Computers).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo provide domain controller-side restrictions for members of the Protected Users security group, the domain functional level must be Windows Server 2012 R2 (or higher). Microsoft Security Advisory \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://msrc-blog.microsoft.com/2014/06/05/an-overview-of-kb2871997/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eKB2871997\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e adds compatibility support for the protections enforced for members of the Protected Users security group for Windows 7, Windows Server 2008 R2, and Windows Server 2012 systems.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp style\u003d\"text-align:left\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSuccessful (Event IDs 303, 304) or failed (Event IDs 100, 104) logon events for members of the Protected Users security group can be recorded on domain controllers within the following event logs:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"text-align:left\"\u003e\n\u003cpre\u003e\u003ccode\u003e%SystemRoot%\\System32\\Winevt\\Logs\\Microsoft-Windows-Authentication%4ProtectedUserSuccesses-DomainController.evtx\u003c/code\u003e\u003c/pre\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\n\u003cpre\u003e\u003ccode\u003e%SystemRoot%\\System32\\Winevt\\Logs\\Microsoft-Windows-Authentication%4ProtectedUserFailures-DomainController.evtx\u003c/code\u003e\u003c/pre\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe event logs are disabled by default and must be enabled on each domain controller. Credential Guard\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor Windows 10 and Windows Server 2016 endpoints, Windows Defender Remote Credential Guard can be leveraged to reduce the exposure of privileged accounts in memory on destination endpoints when Remote Desktop is used for connectivity. With Remote Credential Guard, all credentials remain on the client (origination system) and are not directly exposed to the destination endpoint. Instead, the destination endpoint requests service tickets from the source as needed.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWhen a user logs in via RDP to an endpoint that has Remote Credential Guard enabled, none of the SSPs in memory store the account's clear-text password or password hash. Note that Kerberos tickets remain in memory to allow interactive (and single sign-on [SSO]) experiences from the destination server.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThe Remote Desktop client (origination) host:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli credentials\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan Accounts\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eLocal accounts that exist on endpoints are often a common avenue leveraged by threat actors to laterally move throughout an environment. This tactic is especially impactful when the password for the built-in local administrator account is configured to the same value across multiple endpoints.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo mitigate the impact of local accounts being leveraged for lateral movement, organizations should consider both limiting the ability of local administrator accounts to establish remote connections and creating unique and randomized passwords for local administrator accounts across the environment.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/help/2871997/microsoft-security-advisory-update-to-improve-credentials-protection-a\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eKB2871997\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e introduced two well-known SIDs that can be leveraged within GPO settings to restrict the use of local accounts for lateral movement.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eS-1-5-113: NT AUTHORITY\\Local account\u003c/code\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eS-1-5-114: NT AUTHORITY\\Local account and member of Administrators group\u003c/code\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSpecifically, the SID \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eS-1-5-114: NT AUTHORITY\\Local account and member of Administrators group\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e is added to an account's access token if the local account is a member of the \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eBUILTIN\\Administrators\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e group. \u003c/span\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eThis is the most beneficial SID to leverage to help stop a threat actor (or ransomware variant) that propagates using credentials for any local administrative accounts.\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNote:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor SID \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eS-1-5-114: NT AUTHORITY\\Local account and member of Administrators group\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, if Failover Clustering is used, this feature should leverage a nonadministrative local account (\u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCLIUSR\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e) for cluster node management. \u003c/span\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIf this account is a member of the local Administrators group on an endpoint that is part of a cluster, blocking the network logon permissions can cause cluster services to fail.\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Be cautious and thoroughly test this configuration on servers where Failover Clustering is used.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eStep 1 – Option 1: S-1-5-114 SID\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo mitigate the use of local administrative accounts from being used for lateral movement, use the \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSID S-1-5-114: NT AUTHORITY\\Local account and member of Administrators group\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e within the following settings:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cem\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eComputer Configuration \u0026gt; Policies style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDeny access to this computer from the network (\u003ccode\u003eSeDenyNetworkLogonRight\u003c/code\u003e)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDeny logon as a batch job network-based logons. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo leverage this configuration via a GPO style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSearch for remote logon attempts for local accounts on an endpoint.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003c/tbody\u003e\n\u003c/table\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003cdiv
