accounts\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eActive Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) Protections\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eActive Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) is Microsoft's implementation of Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) and integrates directly with Active Directory forests and domains. It can be utilized for a variety of purposes, including digital signatures and user authentication. Certificate Templates are used in AD CS to issue certificates that have been preconfigured for particular tasks. They contain settings and rules that are applied to incoming certificate requests and provide instructions on how a valid certificate request is provided.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIn June of 2021, SpecterOps published a blog post named \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://specterops.io/blog/2021/06/17/certified-pre-owned/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCertified Pre-Owned\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, which details their research into possible attacks against AD CS. Since that publication, Mandiant has continued to observe both threat actors and red teamers enhance targeting of AD CS in support of post-compromise objectives. Mandiant's \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/defend-ad-cs-threats/\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eblog post\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e \u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eand \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://services.google.com/fh/files/misc/active-directory-certificate-services-hardening-wp-en.pdf\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ehardening guide\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e address the continued abuse scenarios and AD CS attack vectors identified through our frontline observations of recent security breaches.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDiscover Vulnerable Certificate Templates\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCertificate templates that have been configured and published by AD CS are stored in Active Directory as objects with an object class of \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003epKICertificateTemplate\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e and can be discovered by blue teams as well as threat actors. Any account that is authenticated to Active Directory can query LDAP directly, with the built-in Windows command \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ecertutil.exe\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, or with specialized tools such as \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://github.com/GhostPack/PSPKIAudit\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePSPKIAudit\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://github.com/ly4k/Certipy\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCertipy\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, and \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://github.com/GhostPack/Certify\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCertify\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e. Mandiant recommends using one of these methods to discover vulnerable certificate templates.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHarden Vulnerable Certificate Templates\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOnce discovered, vulnerable certificate templates should be hardened to prevent abuse.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003col\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnsure that all domain controllers and Certificate Authority servers are patched with the latest updates and hotfixes.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAfter installing Windows update (\u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://support.microsoft.com/en-us/topic/kb5014754-certificate-based-authentication-changes-on-windows-domain-controllers-ad2c23b0-15d8-4340-a468-4d4f3b188f16\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eKB5014754\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e) and monitoring/remediating for Event IDs 39 and 41, configure Active Directory to support full enforcement mode to reject authentications based on weaker mappings in certificates.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eUsing one of the aforementioned methods, regularly review published certificate templates, specifically for any settings related to SAN specifications configured in existing templates.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eReview the security permissions assigned to all published certificate templates and validate the scope of enrollment and write permissions are delegated to the correct security principals.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eReview published templates configured with the following Enhanced Key Usages (EKUs) that support domain authentication and verify the operational requirement for these configurations.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAny Purpose (2.5.29.37.0)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSubordinate CA (None)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eClient Authentication (1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.2)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePKINIT Client Authentication (1.3.6.1.5.2.3.4)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSmart Card Logon (1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.2)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eFor templates with sensitive Enhanced Key Usage (EKU), limit enrollment permissions to predefined users or groups, as certificates with EKUs can be used for multiple purposes. Access control lists for templates should be audited to ensure that they align with the principle of least privilege.\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTemplates that allow for domain authentication should be carefully reviewed to verify that built-in groups that contain a large scope of accounts are not assigned enrollment permissions. Example: built-in groups that could increase the risk for abuse include:\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEveryone\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNT AUTHORITY\\Authenticated Users\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDomain Users\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:circle;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDomain Computers\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eWhere possible, enforce \"CA Certificate Manager approval\" for any templates that include a SAN as an issuance requirement. This will require that any certificate issuance requests be manually reviewed and approved by an identity assigned the \"Issue and Manage Certificates\" permission on a certificate authority server.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnsure that Certificate Authorities have not been configured to accept any SAN (irrelevant of the template configuration). This is a non-default configuration and should be avoided wherever possible. This abuse vector is mitigated by KB5014754, but until enforcement of strong mappings is enforced, abuse could still occur based upon historical certificates missing the new OID containing the requester's SID. For additional information, reference the following \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/it-pro/windows-server-2012-r2-and-2012/dn786426(v\u003dws.11)#controlling-user-added-subject-alternative-names\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMicrosoft article\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTreat both root and subordinate certificate authorities as Tier 0 assets and enforce logon restrictions or authentication policy silos to limit the scope of accounts that have elevated access to the servers where certificate services are installed and configured.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAudit and review the NTAuthCertificates container in AD to validate the referenced CA certificates, as this container references CA certificates that enable authentication within AD. Before authenticating a principal, AD checks the NTAuthCertificates container for the CA specified in the authenticating certificate's Issuer field to validate the authenticity of the CA. If rogue or unauthorized CA certificates are present, this could be indicative of a security event that requires further triage and investigation.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo avoid the theft of a CA's private keys (e.g., via the DPAPI backup protocol), protect the private keys by leveraging a Hardware Security Module (HSM) on servers where certificate authority services are installed and configured.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnforce multifactor authentication (MFA) for CA and AD management and operations.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eKeep the root CA offline and use subordinate CAs to issue certificates.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eRegularly validate and identify potential misconfigurations within existing certificate templates using the built-in Windows command \u003c/span\u003e\u003ccode style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ecertutil.exe\u003c/code\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, or with specialized tools such as \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://github.com/GhostPack/PSPKIAudit\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePSPKIAudit\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://github.com/ly4k/Certipy\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCertipy\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e, and \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://github.com/GhostPack/Certify\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCertify\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e. Public tools (e.g., PSPKIAudit, Certipy, or Certify) may be flagged by EDR products as they are frequently used by red teams and threat actors.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo mitigate NTLM Relay attacks in AD CS, enable Extended Protection For Authentication for Certificate Authority Web Enrollment and Certificate Enrollment Web Service. Additionally, require that AD CS accept only HTTPS connections. For additional details, reference the following \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://support.microsoft.com/en-gb/topic/kb5005413-mitigating-ntlm-relay-attacks-on-active-directory-certificate-services-ad-cs-3612b773-4043-4aa9-b23d-b87910cd3429\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMicrosoft Article\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnable audit logging for Certificate Services on CA servers and Kerberos Authentication Service on Domain Controllers by using group policy. Ensure that event IDs 4886 and 4887 from CA servers and 4768 from domain controllers are aggregated in the organization's SIEM solution.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnable the audit filter on each CA server. This is a bitmask value that represents the seven different audit categories that can be enabled; if all values are enabled, the audit filter will have a value of 127.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:decimal;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eLog and monitor events from the CA servers and domain controllers to enhance detections related to AD CS activities (steps 16 and 17 are needed to ensure the appropriate logs are generated).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ol\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCertificate Request with Mismatched SAN (ESC1)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1649/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1649 - Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor event IDs 4886 (certificate request received) and 4887 (certificate issued) on CA servers. Alert when the requesting account's identity differs from the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) specified in the certificate.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eNTLM Relay to AD CS Web Enrollment (ESC8)\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1557/001/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1557.001 - LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1649/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1649 - Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor for NTLM authentication to AD CS HTTP enrollment endpoints from domain controllers or privileged servers. Correlate with PetitPotam coercion indicators. This attack chain provides a direct path from any domain user to Domain Admin.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003c/tbody\u003e\n\u003c/table\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003cdiv align\u003d\"left\" style\u003d\"text-align:center\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline;color:#5f6368;display:block;font-size:16px;font-style:italic;margin-top:8px;width:100%\"\u003eTable 30: Detection opportunities for AD CS abuse\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch3\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePreventing Destructive Actions in Kubernetes and CI/CD Pipelines\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eOrganizations should implement a proactive, defense-in-depth technical hardening strategy to systematically address foundational security gaps and mitigate the risk of destructive actions across their Kubernetes environments and Continuous Integration/Continuous Delivery or Deployment (CI/CD) pipelines. Adversaries increasingly target the CI/CD pipeline and the Kubernetes control plane because they serve as centralized hubs with direct access to application deployments and underlying infrastructure.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSource and Build Compromise:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Threat actors target code repositories (e.g., GitHub, GitLab, Azure DevOps) and build environments to steal injected environment variables and secrets. Attackers can then commit malicious workflow files designed to exfiltrate repository data or deploy unauthorized infrastructure.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eContainer Registry Poisoning: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eBy compromising developer credentials or CI/CD pipeline permissions, attackers overwrite legitimate application images in the container registry. When the Kubernetes cluster pulls the updated image, it unknowingly deploys a poisoned container embedded with backdoors, ransomware, or destructive data-wiping logic.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eCluster-Level Destruction:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Once an attacker gains a foothold inside the Kubernetes cluster, they often abuse over-permissive role-based access control (RBAC) configurations. This provides the capability to execute destructive commands using application programming interfaces (APIs) (e.g., kubectl delete deployments), wipe persistent volumes, or delete critical namespaces, effectively causing a loss of availability and application denial of service.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSecrets Extraction and Lateral Movement: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAttackers routinely execute Kubernetes-specific attack tools to harvest secrets from compromised Kubernetes pods. These secrets often contain database passwords and cloud identity and access management (IAM) keys, allowing the attacker to pivot out of the cluster and impact cloud-based resources.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional information related to \u003ca href\u003d\"https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-ci-cd-security-risks/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003esecuring CI/CD\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eHardening and Mitigation Guidance\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eTo defend against CI/CD compromises and destructive actions within Kubernetes, organizations must enforce strict identity boundaries, cryptographic trust, and a least-privilege architecture.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eIsolate the Kubernetes Control Plane:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Disable unrestricted and public internet access to the Kubernetes API server. For managed services like GKE, EKS, and AKS, ensure the control plane is configured as a private endpoint or heavily restricted via authorized network IP allow-listing. Access to the API should only be permitted from trusted, designated internal management subnets or secure corporate VPNs.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSecure Management Interfaces and CI/CD Pipelines:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e Enforce mandatory MFA for all access to infrastructure management platforms, including source code repositories such as GitLab/GitHub, and container registries. Utilize hardened container images (e.g., Chainguard containers, Docker Hardened Images) as base images. Implement software supply chain security frameworks (like \u003c/span\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://openssf.org/projects/slsa/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eSLSA\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e) by requiring image signing, provenance generation, and admission controllers (such as Binary Authorization). This ensures that the Kubernetes cluster will definitively reject and block any unverified or poisoned container images from running.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnforce Strict RBAC and Least Privilege:\u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e To limit the \"blast radius\" of a compromised pod, restrict the use of the cluster-admin role and strictly prohibit wildcard (*) permissions for standard service accounts. Workloads must run under strict security contexts—blocking containers from executing as root, preventing privilege escalation, and restricting access to the underlying worker node (e.g., disabling hostPID and hostNetwork).\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eImplement Immutable Cluster Backups: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eProtect the cluster's state (etcd) and stateful workload data (Persistent Volumes) by utilizing immutable backup repositories. This ensures that even if an attacker gains administrative access to the cluster or CI/CD pipeline and attempts to maliciously delete all resources, the backups cannot be destroyed or altered.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli style\u003d\"list-style-type:disc;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnable Audit Logging and Threat Detection: \u003c/strong\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eEnsure Kubernetes Control Plane audit logs, node-level telemetry, and CI/CD pipeline logs are actively forwarded to a centralized SIEM. Deploy dedicated container threat detection capabilities to immediately alert on malicious exec commands, suspicious Kubernetes enumeration tools, or bulk data deletion attempts within the pods.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAdditional information related to \u003ca href\u003d\"https://owasp.org/www-project-kubernetes-top-ten/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003esecuring Kubernetes\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch4\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDetection Opportunities for Kubernetes and CI/CD\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h4\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003cdiv align\u003d\"left\"\u003e\n\u003cdiv Case\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMITRE ID\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cstrong style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDescription\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eBulk Kubernetes Resource Deletion\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1485/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1485 - Data Destruction\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor Kubernetes API audit logs for bulk delete operations targeting Deployments, StatefulSets, Persistent Volume Claims, Namespaces, or ConfigMaps.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eUnsigned or Modified Container Image Deployed to Cluster\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1525/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1525 - Implant Internal Image\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor container registries and Kubernetes admission events for deployment of images that fail signature verification, lack provenance attestation, or originate from untrusted registries.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAnomalous Kubernetes Secret Access\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1552/007/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1552.007 - Unsecured Credentials: Container API\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor Kubernetes audit logs for API calls to \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e/api/v1/secrets\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e or \u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"font-style:italic;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e/api/v1/namespaces/*/secrets\u003c/span\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003e from service accounts or users that do not normally access secrets. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAlert on bulk secret enumeration and on access to secrets in sensitive namespaces.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eUnauthorized Modification to CI/CD Pipeline Configuration\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1195/002/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1195.002 - Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor source code repositories for modifications to CI/CD pipeline configuration files. \u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eAlert on changes to pipeline definitions made by accounts that are not members of designated pipeline-owner groups, or changes pushed code outside of an approved pull request/merge request workflow.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003ePrivileged Container or Host Namespace Access\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1611/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1611 - Escape to Host\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor Kubernetes audit logs for pod creation or modification events requesting privileged security contexts, host namespace access, or volume mounts to sensitive host paths. These configurations allow container escape and direct access to the underlying worker node. Alert on any workload requesting these capabilities outside or pre-approved system namespaces.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003ctr\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eKubernetes Audit Logging or Security Agent Tampering\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href\u003d\"https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1562/007/\" rel\u003d\"noopener\" target\u003d\"_blank\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"text-decoration:underline;vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eT1562.007 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Cloud Firewall\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003ctd style\u003d\"vertical-align:middle;border:1px solid #000000;padding:16px\"\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eMonitor for modifications to Kubernetes API server audit policy configurations, deletion or redirection of log export sinks, and disablement or removal of container runtime security agents. Alert on changes to cluster-level logging configurations in managed services (GKE Cloud Audit Logs, EKS Control Plane Logging, AKS Diagnostic Settings) including disablement of API server, authenticator, or scheduler log streams.\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003c/td\u003e\n\u003c/tr\u003e\n\u003c/tbody\u003e\n\u003c/table\u003e\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003cdiv align\u003d\"left\" style\u003d\"text-align:center\"\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline;color:#5f6368;display:block;font-size:16px;font-style:italic;margin-top:8px;width:100%\"\u003eTable 31: Detection opportunities for Kubernetes and CI/CD\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/div\u003e"]]],[null,null,[null,[null,"\u003ch3\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eConclusion\u003c/span\u003e\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cspan style\u003d\"vertical-align:baseline\"\u003eDestructive attacks, including ransomware, pose a serious threat to organizations.
